one_line_takeaway: Spike-PTSD adds a PTSD-neuroscience-inspired spike-scaling loss term (amplifying or suppressing specific hyper/hypo-activated neurons identified via a class-neuron correlation matrix) on top of standard FGSM/PGD adversarial optimization, pushing white-box targeted-attack ASR against rate-coding SNNs from near-zero (prior SOTA) to 77-99.9% across six datasets and four architectures.
key_finding: Spike-PTSD achieves over 99% ASR in most of the 16 dataset/model/encoding configurations in Table 1 and consistently outperforms all three SOTA baselines (RGA, HART, PDSG) across both targeted and untargeted settings and across static and dynamic (neuromorphic) datasets. The most pronounced gap is in the targeted-attack setting: the baselines largely collapse (RGA and PDSG are frequently at or near 0% ASR, e.g. PDSG is exactly 0% in 13 of the 16 rows shown in Table 1, RGA ranges roughly 0-63.24%, HART ranges roughly 0.92-69.56%), whereas Spike-PTSD's targeted ASR ranges from 77.06% (SVHN, VGG16, E2, y_t=1) up to 99.87% (CIFAR100, Res18, E1, y_t=15). For untargeted attacks Spike-PTSD's ASR ranges from 42.28% (SVHN, VGG16, E2) up to 99.86% (SVHN, VGG16, E1), also generally exceeding the baselines, though the margin is narrower than in the targeted setting and on some Poisson-encoded (E2) configurations the improvement over HART is more modest (e.g. Res18/CIFAR10/E2 untargeted: Spike-PTSD 56.23% vs HART 30.07%). Ablations show the novel spike-scaling loss term is the main driver of the ASR gains rather than the underlying FGSM/PGD perturbation search itself.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Spike-PTSD, a white-box adversarial example attack (AEA) framework against Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) that is explicitly biologically inspired by abnormal neuronal firing observed in Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)-affected brains (hyperactivation in the amygdala for targeted misbehaviour, hypoactivation/dysregulation in the mPFC). The framework has three modules run in sequence: (1) Spike-Brain Profiling (SBP), which hooks spike activation maps at each spike hidden layer, detects 'spike vanishing', and prunes those layers to select a candidate target layer l_t; (2) Injured Neuron Identification (INI), which builds a class-neuron correlation matrix Theta from the test set to select a target class y_t and target neurons N_t for targeted attacks (mirroring amygdala hyperactivation), or selects high-spike-rate neurons above the layer average r_avg to suppress for untargeted attacks (mirroring mPFC hypoactivation), justified by a 'significance condition' D_r > D_lt; (3) Adversarial Perturbation Optimization (APO), which adds a new spike-scaling loss term L_spike (MSE between the target neurons' spike map and a target spike rate r_t = 1 for amplification/targeted, or r_t = 0 for suppression/untargeted) to the standard adversarial cross-entropy loss L_adv, combined into L_ptsd with tunable coefficients (gamma, beta for targeted; lambda, mu for untargeted), and optimized via existing FGSM/PGD iterative perturbation search. The authors state this is, to their knowledge, the first attempt to bridge neuroscience (PTSD neural abnormality) and adversarial machine learning, and that the spike-scaling objective is a universal add-on that can combine with any existing ANN-style adversarial loss to make it SNN-specific.
threat_model: White-box attacker targeting rate-coding SNNs deployed via widely used open-source model-sharing platforms. The attacker is either the party uploading the model or the party downloading it. The attacker has access to internal model information (architecture and spike activation information) and, when acting as a downloader, to the test dataset associated with the victim model, which is used to analyze internal spiking dynamics and craft adversarial examples (AEs). Both targeted and untargeted attack settings are considered. The paper additionally frames a discussion-section (not evaluated) extension toward neuromorphic hardware: spike scaling as modeled by Spike-PTSD could physically manifest as firing-threshold variation, membrane-voltage perturbation, or spike-timing jitter caused by hardware noise, hardware Trojans in synaptic circuits, or maliciously crafted event streams injected via neuromorphic sensors -- this hardware angle is explicitly presented as a discussion/implication, not something the paper implements or measures.
limitations: The paper's own text and results surface several limitations: (1) the significance condition underlying untargeted neuron selection (D_r > D_lt) is verified empirically for the specific models/datasets tested rather than proven to hold generally, and the paper states it 'generally holds for most network architectures' -- an inductive, not universal, claim; (2) the threat model is explicitly white-box, requiring access to model internals and the test dataset, which is a strong attacker assumption; (3) results on Poisson encoding (E2) and on some SVHN/CIFAR10 configurations show comparatively lower untargeted ASR (as low as 42-56%) than the near-100% figures highlighted in the abstract, indicating the '99% success rate' headline does not hold uniformly across all settings; (4) the ablation on target-layer depth found DVS128Gesture behaves as an outlier due to an extreme imbalance in target-neuron counts across layers, suggesting the layer-selection heuristic is sensitive to architecture-specific neuron distribution; (5) the hardware/neuromorphic-chip security implications (Section 4.5.3) are presented only as discussion and future direction -- no hardware experiments (e.g. on Loihi or TrueNorth silicon, or under actual hardware Trojans/bit-flip attacks) were conducted in this paper; (6) the authors explicitly flag as future work extending Spike-PTSD into a trigger-generation mechanism for stealthy Trojan/backdoor attacks and integrating it into neuromorphic EDA/design-automation flows, meaning the Trojan/backdoor angle mentioned in the abstract text fragment is not evaluated here, only proposed as future work.

quantitative_results: Table 1 targeted ASR, CIFAR10/Res18/E1/y_t=6: Spike-PTSD 99.82% (+/-0.1) vs RGA 0.5%, HART 41.65%, PDSG 0%
Table 1 untargeted ASR, CIFAR10/Res18/E1/y_t=6: Spike-PTSD 99.13% (+/-0.7) vs RGA 74.47%, HART 88.47%, PDSG 62.87%
Table 1 targeted ASR, SVHN/VGG16/E2: Spike-PTSD 91.67% (+/-0.7) vs RGA 0.26%, HART 9.28%, PDSG 0%
Table 1 untargeted ASR, SVHN/VGG16/E2: Spike-PTSD 42.28% (+/-0.7) vs RGA 16.34%, HART 27.02%, PDSG 10.14% (lowest Spike-PTSD untargeted result in the table)
Table 1 targeted ASR, N-MNIST/VGGDVS/E3/y_t=0: Spike-PTSD 99.6% (+/-0.3) vs RGA 0.38%, HART 0.92%, PDSG 0%

Table 1 targeted ASR, N-MNIST/ResDVS/E3/y_t=0: Spike-PTSD 92.13% (+/-0.5), the only Spike-PTSD targeted result under 95% besides the SVHN and CIFAR100(E1,y_t=15 gets 99.87) cases -- lowest overall together with Gesture/ResDVS 92.13%
Ablation (Fig. 5b): combined targeted loss with gamma=1,beta=1 raises ASR from 7.7% to 98.07% (also stated as 15.87% to 98.07% in a related configuration)
Ablation (Fig. 5b): untargeted ASR stays at 97.43% even when the adversarial term is removed (lambda=0), driven by the spike-scaling term alone
Table 2 significance condition: D_r approx 1 for most model/dataset pairs vs D_lt ranging from 0.0877 (N-MNIST/Res19NetDVS) to 0.4601 (CIFAR10-DVS/VGGDVS)
Fig. 9a: Spike-PTSD ASR approaches 100% with increasing optimization iterations across target classes y_t in {0,1,7,8}; baseline RGA improves only from 9.11% to 16.04% over the same iteration range
